name: Media Player Framework
scenario: You're building a media player framework (think VLC, Spotify, YouTube Music). Different media types — Audio, Video, Podcast — all need to be 'played' but do so differently. They all share common infrastructure (title, duration, buffering). The play lifecycle is always the same: load → buffer → start playback → stop. This is a textbook Template Method application: the framework defines WHEN things happen; concrete implementations define HOW. Real media players (VLC, ffmpeg-based tools) use exactly this pattern.
description: Build an abstract media player hierarchy using the Template Method pattern. Abstract MediaPlayer defines the playback lifecycle. Audio, Video, and PodcastPlayer implement type-specific playback details.

core.description: Abstract `MediaPlayer` class: title (String), durationSeconds (int), isBuffering (boolean). Validate title and duration > 0.
MediaPlayer final template `play()`: calls buffer() then loadMedia(), startPlayback(), 'Playing for Xs', stopPlayback(). Concrete `buffer()` prints 'Buffering...'
`AudioPlayer extends MediaPlayer`: quality (String, e.g. '320kbps'). loadMedia, startPlayback, stopPlayback print audio-specific messages.
`VideoPlayer extends MediaPlayer`: resolution (String, e.g. '1080p'). Similar lifecycle methods.
main() creates all three types, stores in MediaPlayer[], calls play() and prints info for each.

bonus.description: Add `PodcastPlayer extends MediaPlayer` with host name field.
Add `abstract double downloadSizeMB()` to MediaPlayer. Audio: durationSeconds * 0.5, Video: durationSeconds * 5.0, Podcast: durationSeconds * 0.3.
Add concrete `totalPlaylistDuration(MediaPlayer[] playlist)` static method that sums durations.
Override `play()` in VideoPlayer to add 'Rendering subtitles...' between buffer and startPlayback. (Requires removing final from play() — design trade-off discussion).

data_to_use:
Create:
- AudioPlayer: 'Blinding Lights', 210 seconds, '320kbps'
- VideoPlayer: 'Java Tutorial', 3600 seconds, '1080p'

Expected main output:
Audio: Blinding Lights (210 s)
Buffering...
Loading audio stream (320kbps)...
▶ Playing audio...
Playing for 210s
■ Audio stopped
---
Video: Java Tutorial (3600 s)
Buffering...
Loading video (1080p)...
▶ Playing video...
Playing for 3600s
■ Video stopped

criteria: Abstract MediaPlayer with correct fields (5 pts)
Template play() method is final (10 pts)
AudioPlayer implements all abstract methods (15 pts)
VideoPlayer implements all abstract methods (15 pts)
Correct methods called for each type (10 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 hints. The template method play() must be final and call the abstract methods in the right order. The abstract methods are what vary between AudioPlayer and VideoPlayer.
submission_instructions: Save as MediaSystem.java. Run it. Push to GitHub — this is the architecture of VLC, Spotify, and any media player framework.
tips_for_success: Design MediaPlayer completely (with all abstract methods declared) before writing subclasses.
play() is `final void play()` — it cannot be overridden by subclasses.
buffer() is concrete in MediaPlayer — same for all media types.
loadMedia(), startPlayback(), stopPlayback() are abstract — each type implements differently.
getMediaType() is abstract — AudioPlayer returns 'Audio', VideoPlayer returns 'Video'.
For getInfo(): `return getMediaType() + ': ' + title + ' (' + durationSeconds + ' s)'`
concepts_reinforced: Abstract class with mixed concrete/abstract methods
Template Method pattern with final algorithm
getInfo() calling abstract getMediaType() — polymorphic concrete method
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this: Real media players use exactly this lifecycle. VLC's libvlc, ffmpeg, Android's MediaPlayer — all define a fixed playback lifecycle that subtypes customize. The abstract class ensures every media type goes through the same steps in the same order. A malformed implementation that skips buffering or forgets to stop would break the framework's contract. By making the template `final`, the framework author says: 'You can change HOW each step works, but you cannot change WHAT steps exist.' This is professional API design.
